title: Christmas cake
subtitle: Aged fruit cake fed with brandy over several weeks
excerpt: A rich, dark fruit cake baked in late autumn and fed weekly with brandy until Christmas. The long storage develops flavour the fresh-baked version cannot achieve: the fruitcake tightens, deepens, and rewards patience.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the rich fruit cake recipe with the method of feeding with spirits. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the overnight fruit soak. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): regional feeding conventions. The Christmas fruitcake tradition in Britain dates to the 17th century, replacing the Twelfth Night cake after the tradition shifted to Christmas Day.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake at least 4 weeks before Christmas. Prick the top and base with a skewer and feed with 2 tablespoons of brandy or whisky weekly. Wrap tightly in baking paper and foil, then store in a tin between feeds. Marzipan and ice in the week before Christmas.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 130°C. Very low heat for a 23 cm cake; adjust down 10°C for smaller, deeper tins.

projectSchedule.title: Bake the cake
projectSchedule.body: Soak the fruit overnight, mix the batter, and bake for 4 hours. While still warm, prick all over and feed with 2 tablespoons of brandy. Wrap tightly and store in a tin.

projectSchedule.title: First feed
projectSchedule.body: Unwrap the cake, prick the base and top with a skewer 20–30 times, and spoon over 2 tablespoons of brandy or whisky. Rewrap tightly and return to the tin.

projectSchedule.title: Second feed
projectSchedule.body: Repeat the brandy feed: prick, pour, rewrap. The cake should smell deep and fruity by now.

projectSchedule.title: Third feed
projectSchedule.body: Third and often final feed if you are icing the following week. Alternatively, continue weekly until the week before Christmas.

projectSchedule.title: Marzipan
projectSchedule.body: Brush the top of the cake with warm apricot jam, roll out 500 g marzipan to the size of the cake, and press it on. Leave to dry for 24 hours before icing.

projectSchedule.title: Ice the cake
projectSchedule.body: Apply royal icing or fondant according to your preferred method. Leave to set before cutting.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pricking a baked fruitcake all over with a skewer and pouring a small amount of spirit (brandy, whisky, or rum) over the surface. The spirit is absorbed slowly into the dense crumb, adding flavour and acting as a preservative. Over several weeks, the feeding transforms a plain fruitcake into something noticeably richer.

content.text: The Christmas cake is a long project, not an afternoon's baking. Bake it in October or November; the weeks of
with brandy are what make it a Christmas cake rather than a fruit cake. The flavour cannot be rushed or imitated by a last-minute soak.

Line the tin thoroughly: a double layer of baking paper on the base and sides, with an extra strip around the outside of the tin tied with string. Four hours at low heat is a long time and the paper insulates the cake from the direct heat of the tin.
The night before: soak the fruit
Combine all the dried fruit and peel in a large bowl, pour over the brandy, stir, and cover with cling film. Leave overnight or for at least 8 hours.

Preheat the oven to 150°C conventional. Line a 23 cm round tin with a double layer of baking paper on the base and a double layer on the sides extending 5 cm above the rim. Wrap a double layer of brown paper or baking paper around the outside of the tin and secure with string. This insulates the cake.

Beat the butter and sugar together until combined (it will not go pale and fluffy with muscovado sugar; that is normal). Beat in the eggs one at a time. Fold in the flour, baking powder, spices, and ground almonds. Stir in the treacle and citrus zests. Add the soaked fruit and chopped almonds and fold to combine evenly. Transfer to the prepared tin and level the surface, making a slight hollow in the centre so the top bakes flat. Bake for 3 hours 45 minutes to 4 hours. After 2 hours, check the top; if it is browning too quickly, lay a loose sheet of foil over it. The cake is done when a skewer comes out clean and the surface feels firm when you press the centre.

While the cake is still warm, prick the top and base all over with a skewer and spoon 2 tablespoons of brandy over the surface. Leave to cool completely in the tin. Once cold, wrap the cake tightly in a double layer of baking paper and then foil, and store in a tin. Feed weekly until the week before Christmas: prick, spoon over 2 tablespoons of spirit, rewrap. Apply marzipan and icing in the final week.
